section.title: The Wedding Banquet and the Great Commandment
section.sectionRationale: The temple confrontations continue. Jesus tells of a wedding banquet whose invited guests refuse and are replaced, yet a guest without wedding clothes is cast out. Then three groups try to trap Him, on taxes, resurrection, and the greatest commandment, and He turns the tables with a question about the Christ that silences them all.
section.thesis: God's kingdom is a gracious feast that the invited spurn and outsiders fill, though none may come on their own terms; and the Christ who answers every trap, upholding God, government, resurrection, and love, is Himself David's Lord, greater than any expected.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the parable of the wedding banquet; the question about taxes to Caesar; the Sadducees on the resurrection; the greatest commandment; and Jesus' own question, whose son is the Christ.

verses.text: Once again, Jesus spoke to them in parables:
“The kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding banquet for his son.
He sent his servants to call those he had invited to the banquet, but they refused to come.

Again, he sent other servants and said, ‘Tell those who have been invited that I have prepared my dinner. My oxen and fattened cattle have been killed, and everything is ready. Come to the wedding banquet.’
But they paid no attention and went away, one to his field, another to his business.
The rest seized his servants, mistreated them, and killed them.
The king was enraged, and he sent his troops to destroy those murderers and burn their city.
Then he said to his servants, ‘The wedding banquet is ready, but those I invited were not worthy.
Go therefore to the crossroads and invite to the banquet as many as you can find.’
So the servants went out into the streets and gathered everyone they could find, both evil and good, and the wedding hall was filled with guests.
But when the king came in to see the guests, he spotted a man who was not dressed in wedding clothes.
‘Friend,’ he asked, ‘how did you get in here without wedding clothes?’ But the man was speechless.
Then the king told the servants, ‘Tie him hand and foot, and throw him into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.’
For many are called, but few are chosen.”

units.label: Taxes to Caesar
units.note: give to God what is God's

verses.text: Then the Pharisees went out and conspired to trap Jesus in His words.

They sent their disciples to Him along with the Herodians. “Teacher,” they said, “we know that You are honest and that You teach the way of God in accordance with the truth. You seek favor from no one, because You pay no attention to external appearance.
So tell us what You think: Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not?”
But Jesus knew their evil intent and said, “You hypocrites, why are you testing Me?
Show Me the coin used for the tax.” And they brought Him a denarius.
“Whose {{eikon|image}} is this,” He asked, “and whose inscription?”
“Caesar’s,” they answered. So Jesus told them, “Give to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s.”
And when they heard this, they were amazed. So they left Him and went away.
That same day the Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, came to Jesus and questioned Him.
“Teacher,” they said, “Moses declared that if a man dies without having children, his brother is to marry the widow and raise up offspring for him.
Now there were seven brothers among us. The first one married and died without having children. So he left his wife to his brother.
The same thing happened to the second and third brothers, down to the seventh.
And last of all, the woman died.
In the resurrection, then, whose wife will she be of the seven? For all of them were married to her.”
Jesus answered, “You are mistaken because you do not know the Scriptures or the power of God.
In the resurrection, people will neither marry nor be given in marriage. Instead, they will be like the angels in heaven.
But concerning the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what God said to you:
‘I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob’? He is not the God of the dead, but of the living.”
When the crowds heard this, they were astonished at His teaching.
And when the Pharisees heard that Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, they themselves gathered together.
One of them, an expert in the law, tested Him with a question:
“Teacher, which commandment is the greatest in the Law?”
Jesus declared, “‘{{agapao|Love}} the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.’
This is the first and greatest commandment.
And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’
All the Law and the Prophets hang on these two commandments.”
While the Pharisees were assembled, Jesus questioned them:
“What do you think about the Christ? Whose son is He?” “David’s,” they answered.
Jesus said to them, “How then does David in the Spirit call Him ‘Lord’? For he says:
‘The Lord said to my {{kyrios|Lord}}, “Sit at My right hand until I put Your enemies under Your feet.”’
So if David calls Him ‘Lord,’ how can He be David’s son?”
No one was able to answer a word, and from that day on no one dared to question Him any further.

eikon.gloss: Image, likeness. The coin bears Caesar's image and so is his; the implied contrast is that human beings bear God's image and so belong wholly to God. Give Caesar his coin, but give God your very self.

agapao.gloss: To love with the will and the whole self, a committed, self-giving love. The greatest commandment is to love God with heart, soul, and mind, and the second to love the neighbor as oneself; on this love the whole Law and Prophets hang.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master. In Psalm 110 David calls the Christ my Lord, so the Messiah is not merely David's descendant but David's sovereign; the title points to Jesus' divine identity, greater than the leaders' expectation of a merely human son of David.

basic.context: Jesus tells of a king whose wedding banquet for his son is spurned by the invited, who even kill his servants, so he judges them and fills the hall with people from the streets. Yet a guest without wedding clothes is bound and cast out. Then three delegations test Jesus: the Pharisees and Herodians on paying taxes to Caesar, the Sadducees on the resurrection, and a lawyer on the greatest commandment. He answers each perfectly and then asks His own question: how can the Christ be both David's son and David's Lord? No one can answer, and they stop questioning Him.
basic.hermeneutics: The banquet parable continues the theme of Israel's leaders rejecting God's invitation while outsiders are gathered, but the wedding garment warns that grace is not license: none may come on their own terms. The three tests show Jesus' wisdom and authority; His counter-question reveals that the Messiah is more than a human king. Read Give to Caesar carefully: it distinguishes limited human authority from God's total claim.
basic.originalLanguages: The coin bears Caesar's eikon, image; people bear God's, and owe Him themselves. The greatest command is to agapao, to love God with the whole self and the neighbor as oneself. And David calls the Christ kyrios, Lord, so the Messiah is David's sovereign, not merely his descendant.
basic.typology: The wedding banquet fulfills the prophetic image of God's feast for His people and the messianic marriage supper. The wedding garment pictures the righteousness God provides. Psalm 110's Lord at God's right hand is fulfilled in the exalted Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus is the King's Son whose wedding is the feast, the wise teacher who cannot be trapped, and the Christ who is David's Lord, seated at God's right hand. He upholds God's total claim, the reality of resurrection, and the primacy of love.
basic.god: God spreads a lavish feast and graciously gathers the unlikely, yet He is holy and will not be approached on human terms. He is the living God of the patriarchs, the God of resurrection, and the One who claims wholehearted love and rightly owns those made in His image.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 25 pictures God's feast for all peoples. Exodus 3 grounds the resurrection in I am the God of Abraham. Deuteronomy 6 and Leviticus 19 give the two great commands. Psalm 110 is the Lord at God's right hand.
basic.oneStory: In His last temple debates, Jesus reveals the kingdom as a feast the invited refuse and outsiders fill, warns that none may come uncovered, and shows Himself wiser than every trap. His final question unveils who He is: the Christ is David's Lord, God's own King at His right hand. The passion approaches, and the enemies fall silent before the One they are about to crucify, who is greater than they ever imagined.

context.literary: This is the third of Jesus' judgment parables against the leaders, followed by a series of hostile test questions that fail one after another, capped by Jesus' own unanswerable question. The sequence displays His total mastery in the temple and sets up the woes of chapter 23 and the Olivet Discourse. The banquet echoes the earlier rejection parables while adding the sober note of the wedding garment.
context.historical: A royal wedding feast was the height of honor; to refuse a king's invitation was a grave insult, and to kill his envoys, treason. The burning of the city hints at the fall of Jerusalem in AD 70. Paying tribute to Caesar with a coin bearing his image and a divine inscription was politically and religiously fraught. The Sadducees denied the resurrection and accepted mainly the books of Moses, hence Jesus' appeal to Exodus. Levirate marriage came from Deuteronomy 25.
context.canonical: The banquet fulfills Isaiah 25:6-9, God's feast for the nations, and anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9. The resurrection argument rests on Exodus 3:6. The great commandments join Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18. The climactic question quotes Psalm 110:1, the most cited Old Testament verse in the New Testament, of Christ enthroned at God's right hand.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn that God's gracious invitation, spurned by the privileged, is opened to all yet must be received on His terms, and to display Jesus' unassailable wisdom and His identity as the divine Christ, David's Lord, worthy of wholehearted love.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The banquet describes God's judgment and grace and prescribes that we come to the feast clothed in His provision, not our own terms. The answers on Caesar, resurrection, and love are prescriptive: honor legitimate authority, give God your whole self, trust the resurrection, and love God and neighbor.

debates.question: What do the wedding clothes represent?

views.label: The righteousness God provides
views.case: The garment is something the king supplies or expects, picturing the imputed righteousness of Christ or the new life God gives; the man is speechless because he presumed to enter on his own terms without what God provides.

views.label: The fruit of genuine repentance and faith
views.case: The garment stands for a life transformed by grace, the good works and changed character that mark a true guest; being in the hall is not enough, for God looks for the reality of faith working itself out, not mere presence.

debates.bearing: Both agree that being outwardly among the gathered is not sufficient; one must be truly clothed by God's grace, whether stressed as His provided righteousness or the transformed life it produces.

debates.question: What does give to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's establish?

views.label: Legitimate but limited civil authority
views.case: Government has real, God-ordained claims, including taxes, that Jesus affirms; believers owe honor and payment to the state in its proper sphere, as Romans 13 later teaches.

views.label: God's total and higher claim
views.case: The emphasis falls on the second half: since humans bear God's image, everything ultimately belongs to God; Caesar's sphere is real but bounded, and where the two conflict, God must be obeyed rather than men.

debates.bearing: Together they set civil authority in its proper, limited place under the supreme claim of God, who owns those made in His image entirely.

study.originalLanguages: The tribute coin carries Caesar's eikon, his image, and so is rightly rendered to him; but the unspoken point is sharper, for human beings carry God's image and therefore owe God themselves. Asked for the greatest command, Jesus answers with agapao, love: love God with all your heart, soul, and mind, and love your neighbor as yourself, the two commands on which all the Law and Prophets hang. Then He presses His own question from Psalm 110: David, in the Spirit, calls the Christ his kyrios, his Lord, so the Messiah is not merely David's son but David's sovereign. The debates end with the leaders silenced before the One who is both son of David and Lord of David.

typology.type: The wedding banquet of the king's son
typology.fulfillment: The messianic feast, fulfilled in the marriage supper of the Lamb, Isaiah 25:6-9, Revelation 19:6-9.
The righteousness God provides in Christ, the clean clothing of the redeemed, Isaiah 61:10, Revelation 19:8.

typology.type: The Lord at God's right hand
typology.fulfillment: The exalted Christ enthroned and awaiting the subjection of His enemies, Psalm 110:1, Hebrews 1:13.

study.christ: Jesus is the King's Son at the center of the feast, the bridegroom whose wedding is the joy of the kingdom. He is the peerless teacher whom no trap can catch, upholding God's total claim over Caesar's limited one, defending the resurrection from the Scriptures and the power of God, and summing all the commandments in love. And in His own question He unveils Himself: the Christ is David's Lord, seated at God's right hand, so that the Messiah the leaders await is far greater than a merely human king. He is the divine Son they are about to reject, yet whose enemies will be put beneath His feet.
study.god: God is a generous King who prepares a lavish feast and, when the invited spurn Him, throws open the doors to gather the unlikely from the streets, both evil and good. Yet He is holy and will not be approached carelessly; those who come must be clothed in what He provides. He is the living God, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, who by that very covenant name guarantees the resurrection, for He is not the God of the dead but of the living. He claims the whole heart of those made in His image, and He has enthroned His Christ at His right hand until every enemy is subdued.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's feast of rich food for all peoples, behind the wedding banquet.
I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the ground of Jesus' argument for the resurrection.
Love the LORD your God with all your heart, the first and greatest commandment.
The LORD said to my Lord, sit at My right hand, the text revealing the Christ as David's Lord.

study.oneStory: In His final temple debates, Jesus reveals the kingdom as a feast the privileged refuse and outsiders fill, warns that none may enter uncovered, and answers every hostile test with unassailable wisdom. Then His own question unveils who He is: the Christ is David's Lord, God's King enthroned at His right hand. The enemies fall silent, and the passion draws near. The One they will crucify is greater than they dreamed, the divine Son who spreads the feast, and the story presses on toward the cross and the empty tomb that will seat Him at the Father's right hand.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the wedding banquet teach about God's invitation?

options.text: The privileged who spurn it are judged, and outsiders are gathered, yet none may come on their own terms.
options.note: The refused invitation, the gathered outsiders, and the wedding garment together carry the point.

options.text: God will fill His kingdom with anyone at all, no questions asked.
options.note: He gathers freely, but the wedding garment shows entry is not without His provision, which this ignores.

options.text: Only the originally invited guests could ever attend.
options.note: The originally invited are replaced when they refuse; others are brought in.

options.text: The banquet was cancelled when the guests refused.
options.note: The banquet goes ahead, filled with new guests.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean by give to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's?

options.text: Civil authority has real but limited claims, while God, whose image we bear, claims us wholly.
options.note: The coin bears Caesar's image; humans bear God's, owing Him everything.

options.text: Believers should pay their taxes to the government.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the greater half, God's total claim on the image-bearer.

options.text: Government and God have completely equal claims on us.
options.note: Caesar's sphere is real but bounded; God's claim is supreme, not merely equal.

options.text: We owe nothing at all to human governments.
options.note: Jesus affirms giving Caesar his due, not refusing it.

quiz.q: How did Jesus prove the resurrection to the Sadducees?

options.text: God calls Himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, so He is the God of the living.
options.note: Verses 31-32 argue from Exodus 3:6 that the patriarchs live to God.

options.text: He pointed to the many prophets who spoke of a future resurrection.
options.note: Other texts teach resurrection, but Jesus deliberately argued from Moses, whom the Sadducees accepted.

options.text: He simply asserted it without any argument.
options.note: He gave a reasoned argument from Scripture and God's power, not a bare assertion.

options.text: He agreed the woman would be wife to all seven.
options.note: He said there is no marriage in the resurrection, correcting their premise.

quiz.q: What are the two greatest commandments?

options.text: Love God with your whole self, and love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: Verses 37-40 give the two commands on which all the Law and Prophets hang.

options.text: Love God with all your heart, above all else.
options.note: That is the first and greatest, but Jesus joins the second, love of neighbor, as inseparable.

options.text: Keep the Sabbath and honor your parents.
options.note: These are commandments, but not the two Jesus names as greatest.

options.text: Love yourself first so you can love others.
options.note: The command is to love God supremely and the neighbor as oneself, not self first.

quiz.q: What did Jesus reveal by asking how the Christ can be both David's son and David's Lord?

options.text: The Messiah is more than a human descendant of David; He is David's divine Lord.
options.note: Verses 43-45 use Psalm 110 to show the Christ's greater, divine identity.

options.text: The Christ would come from the line of David.
options.note: True, but Jesus' point presses past it: David also calls Him Lord, revealing more than descent.

options.text: David was confused about who the Messiah would be.
options.note: David spoke by the Spirit; the tension is intended to reveal the Christ's identity, not David's confusion.

options.text: The Christ has no connection to David at all.
options.note: Jesus affirms He is David's son as well as David's Lord.

questions: The invited guests were simply too busy with field and business to come. What good and ordinary things most easily crowd out your response to God's invitation?
The man without wedding clothes tried to come on his own terms. Are you trusting the covering God provides in Christ, or something of your own?
You bear God's image and so belong wholly to Him. What part of your life are you still handing to Caesar that really belongs to God?
Jesus said all the Law hangs on loving God and neighbor. If your week were measured by those two loves, what would it reveal?
The Christ is David's Lord, greater than any human king. Where do you need to enlarge your view of who Jesus is?

translationNotes.note: For many are called, but few are chosen closes the parable; called here is the wide gospel invitation, while chosen marks those who truly receive it and are clothed for the feast.
Jesus quotes Exodus 3:6; the argument turns on the present tense, I am the God of Abraham, showing the patriarchs still live to God, who is not the God of the dead but of the living.
The quotation is Psalm 110:1, where the first Lord is God (Yahweh) and the second is the Messiah (my Lord); the psalm is the most frequently cited Old Testament verse in the New Testament.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
